title: Monetary Policy Report—October 2025
summary: The Bank of Canada's October 2025 Monetary Policy Report details a Canadian economy adjusting to steep US tariffs, which have led to falling demand for Canadian goods, slower growth, and slumped exports and business investment. The Bank simultaneously lowered its policy rate by 25 basis points to 2.25% as underlying inflation remains near the 2.5% target while total CPI inflation was 2.4% in September. The outlook projects Canadian economic growth to strengthen from around 0.75% in H2 2025, averaging 1.4% annually over 2026-2027, with inflation expected to remain around 2% over the projection horizon.
key_points: Canadian economic growth has slowed due to disruptive effects of US tariffs and a decline in population growth, with exports and business investment slumping
CPI inflation was 2.4% in September 2025, up from approximately 2% in prior months; underlying inflation continues around 2.5%
The Bank of Canada reduced its target for the overnight rate by 25 basis points to 2.25%, with the Bank Rate at 2.5% and deposit rate at 2.20%
Canada's economic growth projected to strengthen from around 0.75% in H2 2025, averaging 1.4% annually over 2026 and 2027
Ongoing US tariffs and global trade reconfiguration are creating upward cost pressures for businesses while weighing on global growth prospects
The Governing Council includes Tiff Macklem (Governor), Carolyn Rogers (Senior Deputy Governor), Toni Gravelle, Sharon Kozicki, Nicolas Vincent, Rhys Mendes, and Michelle Alexopoulos
why_it_matters: This report provides the authoritative Canadian macroeconomic outlook amid significant trade disruption. The Bank's simultaneous rate cut signals a cautious easing stance—supporting growth while inflation remains near target but with upward cost pressures from tariffs. Market participants tracking CAD, Canadian bonds, and North American trade dynamics need this baseline for forecasting Canadian monetary policy trajectory.
macro_context: The Canadian economy faces a dual challenge: tariff-driven demand shock suppressing growth while simultaneously generating cost-push inflation through supply chain reconfiguration. The Bank's 25bp rate cut represents a measured response, suggesting they view the growth slowdown as the primary near-term risk while maintaining confidence that inflation will remain near the 2% target. Global trade restructuring from US tariff policy is cited as a structural headwind with lasting negative impact on Canadian economic activity.

human_review.notes: Source is a legitimate, high-quality central bank publication with full attribution to Bank of Canada Governing Council. Contains specific numerical projections and policy decisions. Minor limitation: source text is a summary/overview rather than the full report, so some detailed deliberations are not available.

notes: Historical record (>90 days old). This is the Bank of Canada's official quarterly monetary policy report covering October 2025 deliberations. The associated rate decision (25bp cut to 2.25%) is embedded in the report content. Source is comprehensive summary but may not contain full statistical annexes or detailed projection tables found in the PDF version.
